topic.title: Bayes' Rule
topic.question: How does Bayes' rule update beliefs given evidence, and why is the posterior proportional to likelihood times prior?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v3 - one beat one idea + sync enforcement

lesson.title: Bayes' Rule: Updating Beliefs with Evidence
lesson.payoff: A 99%-accurate disease test still has a 91% false-positive rate when the disease is rare -- Bayes' rule is the only tool that reveals this, turning a misleading number into a correct 9% posterior.
staircase: symmetry of the joint: two ways to factor P(H and E)
isolating the posterior by dividing both sides by P(E)
computing P(E) as total evidence via the law of total probability
numeric worked example with a rare disease
conceptual insight: low prior crushes high likelihood
lesson.destination: You will hold P(H|E) = P(E|H)*P(H) / [P(E|H)*P(H) + P(E|not-H)*P(not-H)] and understand that the denominator is the normalizing constant that makes the posterior a valid probability, forcing it to be proportional to likelihood times prior.

narration.fullText: Imagine you test positive for a rare disease. Should you panic? Bayes' rule -- a formula for updating a belief after seeing evidence -- will give you the real answer. Start with two events: H, the hypothesis that you have the disease, and E, the evidence that the test is positive. The conditional probability P(H|E) -- read as the probability of H given E -- is what we want: the chance you are sick given a positive test. To find it, notice that the joint probability P(H and E), meaning both events happen together, can be factored two ways: as P(E|H) times P(H), or equally as P(H|E) times P(E). Setting these equal gives us P(H|E) times P(E) equals P(E|H) times P(H). Divide both sides by P(E) and you have Bayes' rule: P(H|E) equals P(E|H) times P(H) over P(E). The numerator is the likelihood -- P(E|H), the probability the test is positive if you are sick -- multiplied by the prior -- P(H), your baseline belief before the test. The denominator P(E) is the total probability of a positive test regardless of disease status, computed by the law of total probability -- a rule that sums over all mutually exclusive ways an event can occur: P(E) equals P(E|H) times P(H) plus P(E|not-H) times P(not-H). Now plug in numbers. The prior P(H) is 0.001. The likelihood P(E|H) is 0.99. The false-positive rate P(E|not-H) is 0.01. First compute the numerator: 0.99 times 0.001 equals 0.00099. Then the other term: 0.01 times 0.999 equals 0.00999. So P(E) equals 0.00099 plus 0.00999 equals 0.01098. Divide: 0.00099 over 0.01098 equals about 0.09 -- a 9% posterior probability. Despite the test being 99% accurate, the rare prior crushes the likelihood: only about 1 in 11 positive tests reflects a true case. This is why the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior -- P(E) is just a constant that normalizes the product to sum to one across all hypotheses.

beats.text: Imagine you test positive for a rare disease. Should you panic? Bayes' rule -- a formula for updating a belief after seeing evidence -- will give you the real answer.
beats.whyThisBeat: motivation: sets the falsifiable stake and introduces the term Bayes rule

beats.text: Start with two events: H, the hypothesis that you have the disease, and E, the evidence that the test is positive.
beats.whyThisBeat: conditional probability and joint probability: names the two events

beats.text: The conditional probability P(H|E) -- the probability of H given E -- is what we want: the chance you are sick given a positive test.
beats.whyThisBeat: conditional probability and joint probability: defines conditional probability notation

beats.text: The joint probability P(H and E) -- meaning both events happen together -- can be factored two ways: as P(E|H) times P(H), or equally as P(H|E) times P(E).
beats.whyThisBeat: symmetry of the joint: introduces two-way factoring of the joint probability

beats.text: Setting these equal gives P(H|E) times P(E) equals P(E|H) times P(H). Divide both sides by P(E) and you have Bayes' rule: P(H|E) equals P(E|H) times P(H) over P(E).
beats.whyThisBeat: isolating the posterior: derives Bayes rule by algebraic manipulation

beats.text: The numerator is the likelihood -- P(E|H), the probability the test is positive if you are sick -- multiplied by the prior -- P(H), your baseline belief before seeing any evidence.
beats.whyThisBeat: isolating the posterior: names and defines likelihood and prior

beats.text: The denominator P(E) is the total probability of a positive test, computed by the law of total probability -- a rule that sums over all mutually exclusive ways an event can occur. P(E) equals P(E|H) times P(H) plus P(E|not-H) times P(not-H).
beats.whyThisBeat: computing P(E) as total evidence: introduces the law of total probability and the full denominator formula

beats.text: Now plug in numbers. The prior P(H) is 0.001. The likelihood P(E|H) is 0.99. The false-positive rate P(E|not-H) is 0.01.
beats.whyThisBeat: numeric worked example: states the three input values

beats.text: Compute the numerator: 0.99 times 0.001 equals 0.00099. The other term: 0.01 times 0.999 equals 0.00999. So P(E) equals 0.00099 plus 0.00999, which is 0.01098.
beats.whyThisBeat: numeric worked example: computes numerator and normalizing constant

beats.text: Divide: 0.00099 over 0.01098 is about 0.09 -- a 9% posterior probability.
beats.whyThisBeat: numeric worked example: states the posterior result

beats.text: Despite a 99%-accurate test, the rare prior crushes the likelihood -- only about 1 in 11 positive tests reflects a true case.
beats.whyThisBeat: conceptual insight: explains why low prior dominates

beats.text: This is why the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior: P(E) is just a normalizing constant that makes the posterior a valid probability summing to one across all hypotheses.
beats.whyThisBeat: conceptual insight: explains proportionality and the role of the denominator

actions.content: A probability tree rooted at a node labeled Population on the left; two branches go right: top branch labeled H disease with probability 0.001, bottom branch labeled not-H no disease with probability 0.999; from H, two child branches: E given H labeled 0.99 positive and not-E given H labeled 0.01 negative; from not-H, two child branches: E given not-H labeled 0.01 positive and not-E given not-H labeled 0.99 negative
H = disease present    E = test positive

Two overlapping circles on a rectangular background; left circle labeled H disease, right circle labeled E positive test; the overlapping lens region is shaded gray and labeled H and E; outside both circles is labeled neither
underline the equality P(E|H) P(H) = P(H|E) P(E) in blue to show the two-way factoring
box the numerator P(E|H) P(H) in red
underline P(E|not-H) P(not-H) in orange to mark the false-positive contribution

A horizontal bar spanning the full width, partitioned into two segments: a small left segment width proportional to 0.001 labeled P(H)=0.001 shaded dark blue, and a wide right segment width proportional to 0.999 labeled P(not-H)=0.999 shaded light gray; below the bar inside the dark-blue segment a thin sub-bar labeled P(E|H)=0.99 and inside the gray segment a thin sub-bar labeled P(E|not-H)=0.01
circle the result 9% in red to emphasize the counterintuitive answer
Posterior proportional to Likelihood x Prior  (P(E) is just normalization)
box the phrase Likelihood x Prior in green to anchor the proportionality insight
underline the denominator P(E) in orange to label it as the normalizing constant
